Introducing the Frustrated Vortex Nematic Phase—a novel liquid crystal state where inherent elastic frustration drives the spontaneous formation of topologically stabilized vortex arrays, creating a reconfigurable refractive index landscape that produces dynamic optical vortices and enables tunable beam shaping with enhanced nonlinear optical responses.

Introducing the Dynamic Dirac Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal phase where spontaneous, periodic oscillations in molecular orientation generate a Floquet photonic band structure with Dirac cone singularities, enabling ultrafast, reversible modulation of diffraction and refraction for adaptive beam steering and wavelength-selective filtering.

Introducing the Fractal Brush Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where molecules self-assemble into a hierarchically branched, fractal-like network that produces scale-invariant, broadband optical scattering and multi-resonant diffraction patterns tunable via external fields.

Introducing the Magneto‐Optic Gyroidal Phase—a liquid crystal state where chiral mesogens coassemble with magnetic nanoparticles into a continuous three‐dimensional gyroid network, yielding isotropic, tunable photonic bandgaps and dynamic circular polarization control under applied magnetic fields for next‐generation 3D photonic devices.

Introducing the Topologically Reconfigurable Ribbon Phase—a liquid crystal state where mesogens self-organize into interlinked, ribbon-like domains with intrinsic topological protection that enable robust, stimulus-responsive optical diffraction and dynamic polarization conversion for adaptive photonic applications.

Introducing the Intrinsic Hyperbolic Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal phase where highly anisotropic mesogens self-organize into lamellar structures that exhibit hyperbolic dispersion, characterized by opposite signs of dielectric permittivity along perpendicular directions. This spontaneous arrangement enables unique optical phenomena such as negative refraction, sub-diffraction-limit imaging, and dynamically tunable directional light propagation under modest external fields.

Introducing the Soliton Network Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where self-organized, topologically robust solitonic excitations interconnect into a tunable network, enabling localized refractive index modulations that yield reversible, field-controlled light trapping, dynamic diffraction, and adaptive beam shaping.

Introducing the Skyrmionic Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where nanoscale, topologically protected skyrmion textures spontaneously emerge within a nematic matrix, enabling tunable optical chirality, dynamic diffraction, and reconfigurable beam steering through external field modulation.

Introducing the Curvature-Coupled Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where spontaneous curvature in layered mesogen arrangements creates intrinsic, reconfigurable lensing and gradient refractive index effects. This enables dynamic beam shaping and tunable self-focusing, as external fields modulate the curvature to control diffraction and optical phase responses in integrated photonic applications.

Introducing the Anapole Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state in which mesogens self-organize into toroidal, nonradiating configurations that cancel far‐field scattering while amplifying localized, near‐field nonlinear optical resonances. This unique arrangement enables dynamic, low-loss modulation of light with potential applications in stealth photonics and enhanced nonlinear signal processing.

Introducing the Conformal Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where mesogens self-organize into director fields that follow conformal mappings, creating smoothly varying refractive index landscapes that enable dynamic light caustics, adaptive focusing, and tunable diffraction patterns with minimal external driving forces.

Introducing the Nanofluidic Photonic Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state in which self-assembled nanochannels within the nematic matrix serve as tunable waveguides for light. The spatial modulation of molecular alignment forms an interconnected, quasi-one-dimensional network that dynamically confines and steers light, enabling enhanced nonlinear interactions and efficient on-chip optical routing under modest external fields.

Introducing the Acousto‐Optic Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where self‐excited acoustic phonon modes couple with the nematic director field to form dynamic, tunable refractive index gratings. This unique phase enables real‐time sound‐controlled Bragg scattering and adaptive diffraction, paving the way for holographic beam steering and reconfigurable optical filtering applications.

Introducing the Berry Curvature Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where spatially modulated director fields generate intrinsic Berry curvature that imprints tunable geometric (Pancharatnam–Berry) phase profiles onto transmitted light, enabling dynamic beam steering, adaptive focusing, and holographic wavefront shaping with minimal external stimulus.

Introducing the Photonic Ice Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where frustrated, tetrahedral-like interactions drive mesogens to self-organize into an ice-inspired network of topological defects. This unique arrangement produces an isotropic, dynamically tunable photonic bandgap and broadband, angle-insensitive light scattering, enabling reversible control of transparency and diffuse reflection via external stimuli.

Introducing the Multiferroic Diaxial Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where spontaneous ferroelectric and antiferromagnetic orders emerge within a biaxially aligned matrix. This intrinsic dual-order coupling facilitates electric and magnetic field-controlled nonreciprocal birefringence and dynamic circular dichroism, enabling directional light propagation and adaptive polarization modulation for advanced optoelectronic applications.

Introducing the Photoelastic Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state in which mesogens possess intrinsic photoelastic properties that enable light to directly modulate their orientation. In this phase, variations in incident light intensity and polarization alter the local elastic moduli and refractive index landscape, producing self-regulated, adaptive diffraction patterns and tunable birefringence without requiring external electric or magnetic fields. This dynamic, light-responsive behavior offers real-time control over beam propagation and photonic bandgaps for advanced optical switching and adaptive lensing applications.

Introducing the Resonant Harmonic Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal phase in which spontaneous nanoscale inversion symmetry breaking forms a periodic nonlinear polarization pattern, enabling robust second-harmonic generation and efficient frequency conversion. This self-organized director field provides dynamic control of the photonic band structure and nonlinear optical response under modest external stimuli, paving the way for adaptive, low-power nonlinear optical devices.

Introducing the Parity-Time Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where mesogens are engineered to possess intrinsic regions of optical gain and loss that self-balance to establish parity-time symmetry. This unique organization results in tunable exceptional points in the photonic band structure, enabling nonreciprocal light propagation, unidirectional transparency, and adaptive diffraction control with minimal external driving forces.

Introducing the Optomechanically Coupled Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where strong incident light induces localized mechanical stresses that dynamically reorient mesogens to form a self-adaptive photonic lattice with a continuously tunable refractive index. This feedback mechanism enables all-optical beam shaping and real-time holographic modulation without the need for external electrodes.

Introducing the Synaptic Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where selective light exposure imprints a persistent, memory-like reconfiguration of mesogen alignment, creating rewritable waveguide networks that enable non-volatile, programmable optical modulation and adaptive diffraction control.

Introducing the Polaritonic Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where mesogens are engineered to incorporate semiconductor nanostructures, enabling strong coupling with confined optical modes to form exciton‐polariton quasiparticles. This hybrid phase delivers ultrafast, low‐threshold nonlinear optical responses along with externally tunable photonic band structures, paving the way for reconfigurable, all‐optical switching and dynamic light‐matter interaction control in next‐generation optoelectronic devices.

Introducing the Self‐Interferometric Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state in which spontaneous periodic modulations of the director field create intrinsic, distributed optical cavities. These built‐in interferometric layers produce sharp, tunable resonances and adaptive photonic bandgaps, allowing dynamic light filtering and beam shaping with minimal external control.

Introducing the Janus Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where chemically asymmetric mesogens spontaneously segregate into coexisting domains with distinct refractive indices and absorptive properties, forming an intrinsic optical gradient that enables directional, stimulus-responsive beam steering and tunable polarization control.

Introducing the Optical Spin‐Glass Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state in which competing molecular interactions lead to a frozen, disordered director field that locks into multiple metastable configurations. This intrinsically glass-like arrangement produces non‐ergodic, hysteretic refractive index landscapes and broadband, stochastic light scattering, enabling adaptive optical memory and camouflage functionalities with minimal external control.

Introducing the Photovoltaic Nematic Phase—a liquid crystal state where intrinsic photoactive mesogens harvest incident light to generate patterned internal electric fields that in turn reorient the director configuration. This self-regulated mechanism creates dynamically tunable photonic bandgaps and adaptive diffractive properties, enabling all-optical modulation and beam steering without external electrodes.

Invent a new polymerization method..answer: Dual-Modal Resonant Polymerization (DMRP) employs synchronized light pulses and ultrasonic vibrations to generate transient radical species in a cyclic sequence, enabling controlled chain initiation, propagation, and self-termination under ambient conditions.

Electrosynchronous Field Polymerization (EFP) employs a dynamic microelectrode array to produce precisely timed electric field gradients that align monomer molecules and trigger localized radical formation, enabling controlled chain propagation and self-regulated termination under ambient conditions.

Magnetically Orchestrated Nanocatalytic Polymerization (MONP) employs magnetically responsive nanoparticles in a monomer mixture; when subjected to a precisely tuned rotating magnetic field, these nanoparticles generate localized catalytic sites that trigger radical formation, allowing controlled initiation, propagation, and termination under ambient conditions.

Plasma-Activated Microfluidic Polymerization (PAMP) integrates a low-temperature plasma source into a microfluidic reactor to generate reactive species on-demand, enabling highly localized radical initiation and termination for uniform, continuously controlled polymer synthesis.

Piezoelectric-Enhanced Polymerization uses mechanically stressed piezoelectric nanocrystals embedded within a monomer mixture; the induced local electric fields generate radicals in situ, enabling controlled chain initiation, propagation, and termination without additional chemical initiators.

Microwave-Assisted Nanoplasmonic Polymerization (MANP) employs metal–dielectric hybrid nanostructures embedded in a monomer matrix that, when exposed to precisely controlled microwave irradiation, generate localized thermal “hot spots” to trigger confined radical initiation, enabling spatially resolved cascade polymer growth with adjustable kinetics.

Mechanophore-Activated Pressure-Jump Polymerization (MAPJP) employs rapid, localized pressure surges in a microreactor to cleave stress‐sensitive bonds (mechanophores) embedded in functionalized monomers, thereby generating radicals that initiate controlled chain propagation and self-termination without external initiators.

Cascade Enzyme-Mimetic Polymerization (CEMP) employs bio‐inspired synthetic catalysts with multiple active sites that sequentially activate inert monomers via controlled redox cycling, generating reactive intermediates which then propagate chains and self‐terminate under mild, aqueous conditions—mimicking natural enzymatic cascades for precise polymer synthesis.

X-Ray Radiolysis Polymerization (XRP) employs precisely controlled pulses of ionizing x-rays to locally ionize monomers, generating free radicals that initiate and propagate polymer chains with high spatial precision and self-limited termination under ambient conditions.

Terahertz-Driven Resonant Polymerization (TDRP) employs pulsed terahertz radiation to resonantly excite specific vibrational modes in custom-designed monomers, triggering localized bond cleavage that forms radicals to initiate and precisely control polymer chain growth under ambient conditions.

Oscillatory Redox-Driven Polymerization (ORDP) employs a self-sustaining Belousov–Zhabotinsky-type reaction to periodically activate redox‐sensitive monomers, generating transient radicals in a cyclic on–off pattern that autonomously controls chain initiation and termination under mild aqueous conditions.

Bioluminescence‐Triggered Polymerization (BTP) employs engineered luciferase enzymes co‐dissolved with photosensitive initiators in a monomer solution; upon addition of the enzyme substrate, localized bioluminescent light activates the initiators to form radicals on demand, enabling spatiotemporally controlled polymer chain growth under ambient, aqueous conditions without external light sources.

Acoustic Holographic Initiated Polymerization (AHIP) employs a programmable array of acoustic transducers to shape interference patterns in a monomer bath, generating localized cavitation that produces high-energy “hot spots” for precise, catalyst‐free radical initiation and spatially controlled polymer chain growth.

Hydrodynamic Vortex Polymerization (HVP) uses a high-speed swirling microreactor to generate controlled shear forces that mechanically activate tailored, shear‐sensitive monomers—inducing radical formation for precise, self-terminating polymer chain growth under ambient conditions.

Electrochemiluminescence‐Driven Polymerization (ELDP) employs an electrochemically activated luminescent electrode to locally trigger embedded photoinitiators, generating radicals that allow for precise spatiotemporal control over the initiation, propagation, and termination of polymer chains without external light sources.

Optomechanical Torque Polymerization (OTP) employs circularly polarized laser light to exert controlled torsional stress on engineered chiral, mechanosensitive monomers, triggering localized bond cleavage that generates radicals to precisely initiate and regulate polymer chain growth under ambient conditions.

Topological Defect‐Driven Polymerization (TDDP) harnesses a liquid crystalline medium that, upon controlled thermal quenching, forms stable topological defects which locally concentrate and orient reactive monomers, triggering self‐initiated polymerization exclusively at these defect sites to yield spatially patterned polymer networks without added initiators.

Proton Gradient-Directed Polymerization (PGDP) employs engineered proton pump nanostructures embedded in an ionic liquid medium to create localized pH gradients that selectively protonate specially designed monomers, triggering controlled radical formation and spatially patterned chain growth under ambient conditions.

Self-Assembled Microdomain-Initiated Polymerization (SAMP) leverages a tailored blend of amphiphilic monomers that spontaneously phase‐separate into uniform microdomains, each embedding a dormant initiator. Mild thermal activation then selectively releases radicals within these domains, triggering localized chain growth and yielding hierarchically structured polymers with nanoscale tunability—all without requiring external high-energy fields.

Electron Tunnel‐Initiated Polymerization (ETIP) employs nanoengineered electrode junctions immersed in a monomer solution to produce a finely controlled tunneling current. This current induces localized single‐electron transfers that convert monomers into radicals, triggering polymer chain growth with nanometric spatial precision under ambient conditions without conventional initiators.

Spin-Crossover Initiated Polymerization (SCIP) employs monomers engineered with spin‐crossover complexes that, upon exposure to mild thermal or photonic stimuli, switch to a high‐spin state to transiently destabilize nearby bonds and release radicals, thereby autonomously triggering and precisely regulating chain propagation with spatiotemporal control.

Quantum Dot-Enhanced Photothermoelectric Polymerization (QD-PTEP) employs engineered core–shell quantum dots dispersed in a monomer solution; upon near-infrared excitation, these quantum dots generate localized photothermal and thermoelectric gradients that induce rapid, site-specific radical formation, thereby enabling precise, spatiotemporally controlled polymer chain initiation, propagation, and self-termination under ambient conditions.

Triboluminescence‐Activated Polymerization (TAP) exploits the unique light emission of triboluminescent microcrystals dispersed in a monomer matrix; when mechanical agitation (such as grinding or shear forces) is applied, these crystals emit localized bursts of light that activate embedded photoinitiators, thereby triggering controlled radical polymerization with precise spatial resolution under ambient conditions without external light sources.

Electron Spin Resonance-Activated Polymerization (ESRAP) leverages paramagnetic monomers that, when exposed to microwave pulses tuned to their intrinsic electron spin resonance frequencies, undergo targeted bond destabilization to generate radicals—thereby initiating, propagating, and self-terminating polymer chain growth with high spatiotemporal precision under ambient conditions.

Charge‐Transfer Complex Polymerization (CTCP) employs specially designed donor–acceptor monomers that self‐assemble into reversible charge‐transfer complexes. Mild thermal or visible‐light activation induces an intracomplex electron transfer, generating radical ion pairs that initiate controlled chain propagation. The dynamic equilibrium between complex formation and dissociation provides an intrinsic, self‐regulating mechanism for precise polymer growth under ambient conditions.

Photogalvanic-Initiated Polymerization (PGIP) uses a tailored photogalvanic additive dispersed within a monomer solution that, upon illumination, converts light into localized electrical potentials. These potentials drive precise electron transfer reactions in adjacent monomers to generate radicals, thereby initiating, propagating, and self-terminating polymer chains with highly controlled spatiotemporal resolution under ambient conditions without external electrodes.

Microbubble‐Triggered Laser Polymerization (MTLP) employs a dispersion of stabilized microbubbles in a monomer solution that, upon targeted pulsed laser irradiation, implode to create transient, high‐energy hotspots. These localized conditions cleave nearby monomer bonds, generating radicals that initiate precise, self‐terminating chain growth.

Focused Ion Beam-Activated Polymerization (FIBAP) employs a precisely tuned, low-dose ion beam to selectively cleave engineered bonds in monomers, generating localized radicals that trigger controlled chain initiation, propagation, and termination with nanoscale spatial precision under ambient conditions—eliminating the need for traditional chemical initiators.

Photonic Crystal-Enabled Polymerization (PCEP) employs a tunable photonic crystal scaffold immersed in a monomer solution to create localized electromagnetic “hot spots” under broad-spectrum illumination. These hotspots selectively activate embedded photoresponsive monomers to form radicals that initiate polymerization at precise sites, while the evolving polymer alters the local refractive index to self-regulate chain propagation and termination under ambient conditions.

Magnon-Mediated Polymerization (MMP) utilizes a ferromagnetic substrate excited by low-power microwave fields to generate coherent spin waves (magnons) that transfer energy to magnetically sensitized monomers. This energy transfer locally induces bond cleavage, forming radicals that initiate polymer chains with high spatial precision and dynamic control under ambient conditions.

Self-Propelled Nanomotor-Initiated Polymerization (SPNIP) employs active catalytic nanomotors dispersed in a monomer medium; as they autonomously traverse the solution, their surface reactions continuously generate localized radicals that trigger uniform polymer chain initiation and propagation under ambient conditions without external stimuli.

Evaporation‐Driven Capillary Polymerization (EDCP) employs patterned microchannel substrates to induce directed evaporation that concentrates monomers mixed with latent thermal initiators; the localized increase in concentration and slight exothermic heat from solvent loss activates the initiators, generating radicals that trigger spatially defined polymer chain growth under ambient conditions.

Resonant Ion Cyclotron Polymerization (RICP) employs monomers engineered with ionizable groups that, when exposed to a precisely tuned oscillating magnetic field at their specific cyclotron resonance frequency, resonantly energize these groups to trigger selective bond cleavage and radical formation—initiating polymer chain growth with self-regulated propagation and termination under ambient conditions.

Suggest a method to synthesize a molecul.answer: Synthesize a substituted norbornadiene via a Diels–Alder reaction between cyclopentadiene and an appropriately functionalized alkyne, yielding a molecule that reversibly photoisomerizes to quadricyclane to store and release solar energy through bond strain.

Synthesize a photochromic dithienylethene derivative by constructing an open‐form diaryl thiophene framework via a Suzuki–Miyaura cross‐coupling of suitably halogenated thiophenes with arylboronic acids; this compound then undergoes a reversible UV-induced electrocyclization (ring closure) that creates a strained bond for solar energy storage, with visible light (or heat) driving the reverse reaction to release the energy.

Synthesize a dihydroazulene derivative by performing a Knoevenagel condensation between a suitably substituted cyclopentanone and an aromatic aldehyde followed by an intramolecular cyclization to form the dihydroazulene core; upon UV irradiation, this molecule is reversibly photoisomerized to its vinylheptafulvene form, thereby storing solar energy in a strained chemical bond.

Synthesize an azobenzene derivative by first diazotizing a suitably substituted aromatic amine and then coupling the resulting diazonium salt with an electron‐rich aromatic compound to form the azo linkage; the resulting azobenzene can reversibly isomerize between its stable trans form and a higher-energy cis form under UV irradiation, thereby storing solar energy in the strained cis configuration and releasing it upon thermal or visible-light induced reversion.

Synthesize a spiropyran by carrying out an acid‐catalyzed condensation of 2‑hydroxybenzaldehyde with 1,3,3‑trimethyl-2‑methyleneindoline in ethanol; the closed spiropyran reversibly photoisomerizes under UV light to its open merocyanine form (storing energy in its new conjugated bond pattern) and reverts under visible light, thereby functioning as a molecular solar energy storage system.

Synthesize a hemithioindigo derivative by first forming a thioindoxyl intermediate (for example, via the oxidative cyclization of an ortho‐mercaptoacetophenone) and then performing a Knoevenagel condensation with a suitably substituted benzaldehyde to create a conjugated hemithioindigo photoswitch that reversibly isomerizes under visible light, storing solar energy in its strained double bond configuration.

Synthesize a diaryl fulgimide by first condensing a 2‐furancarboxylic anhydride with a substituted benzaldehyde under basic conditions to form a diaryl fulgide, then converting it to its imide form via reaction with a primary amine; this fulgimide undergoes a reversible 6π‐electrocyclization under UV light to form a strained ring that stores solar energy, which reopens upon visible light or heat to release the stored energy.

Synthesize a 9,10‐disubstituted anthracene derivative that undergoes reversible [4+4] photodimerization: start with anthracene, perform a Friedel–Crafts acylation at the 9,10‐positions using an appropriate acid chloride (e.g., 2,4‐dimethylbenzoyl chloride) with AlCl₃ to introduce bulky substituents, then reduce the resulting ketones to yield the dialkylated anthracene; upon UV irradiation, the molecule photodimerizes to form a strained cycloadduct that stores solar energy, which can be reverted thermally or by visible light to release the stored energy.

Synthesize a coumarin dimer by first preparing a substituted coumarin via a Pechmann condensation (for example, reacting resorcinol with ethyl acetoacetate) and then tethering two coumarin units with a short alkylene linker so that under UV irradiation they undergo a reversible [2+2] cycloaddition to form a cyclobutane bridge—storing solar energy in the strained bond that can later be cleaved by irradiation at a different wavelength.

Synthesize an overcrowded alkene molecular motor by first preparing two suitably substituted diaryl ketones and then coupling them via a McMurry reaction to form the overcrowded alkene; upon UV irradiation, this molecule twists into a high‐energy, strained configuration that thermally reverts to the stable form, thereby reversibly storing and releasing solar energy.

Synthesize a ladderene derivative by first preparing a tethered bis(vinyl) precursor and then subjecting it to a UV‐induced intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition to form a strained ladderene framework whose cyclobutane bridges can thermally revert to their open diene form, thereby storing and releasing solar energy reversibly.

Synthesize a substituted benzofulvene via a condensation of cyclopentadiene with an appropriately functionalized benzaldehyde. The resulting benzofulvene is designed to undergo a reversible 6π‐electrocyclization: UV irradiation triggers its cyclization into a strained bicyclic isomer that stores solar energy in the newly formed sigma bond, while subsequent visible‐light exposure or gentle heating reopens the ring to release the stored energy.

Develop a substituted cyclooctatetraene (COT) that, under UV irradiation, reversibly isomerizes to a strained norcaradiene form. In practice, one would first prepare a suitably functionalized linear polyene precursor and then employ ring‐closing metathesis (using, for example, a Grubbs catalyst) to form the eight‐membered COT ring. Installing electron‐withdrawing groups at strategic positions further biases the system so that UV light drives a valence isomerization to a high‐energy norcaradiene featuring a strained cyclopropane-like bond; subsequent thermal treatment reverses the reaction, releasing the stored solar energy.

Synthesize a donor–acceptor Stenhouse adduct by condensing a furfural derivative with an active methylene compound (such as barbituric acid) in the presence of a tertiary amine to yield an open‐chain, highly conjugated triene that, upon visible‐light irradiation, reversibly cyclizes into a strained ring—storing solar energy in the newly formed chemical bond.

Prepare stiff‐stilbene by subjecting 1‑indanone to McMurry coupling (using TiCl₄ and Zn in THF) to yield 1,1′‑biindanylidene—a rigid photoswitch that, upon UV exposure, isomerizes from its low‐energy E form to a strained, high‐energy Z form, thereby storing solar energy in the twisted double bond configuration which is released upon thermal reversion.

Synthesize an intramolecular oxetane using a Paterno–Büchi reaction: prepare an o‑alkenylbenzophenone derivative where a benzophenone carbonyl and a tethered alkene are poised for intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition; under UV light the excited carbonyl adds to the alkene to form a strained oxetane ring that stores solar energy, which can then be reversed (thermally or by visible light) to release the energy.

Synthesize a spirooxazine derivative by condensing 2‑hydroxy‑1‑naphthaldehyde with a suitably substituted o‑aminophenol under mild acid catalysis to form the closed spirooxazine; upon UV irradiation, this molecule undergoes a reversible ring‑opening to a high‑energy merocyanine isomer (storing solar energy in its strained oxazine bond), with visible light or gentle heating driving the re‑closure and energy release.

Develop a vinyl‐substituted BODIPY photoswitch as follows: First, synthesize a conventional BODIPY core by condensing 2,4‐dimethylpyrrole with an aromatic aldehyde under acidic conditions, oxidizing the intermediate (e.g. with DDQ), and complexing with BF₃·OEt₂. Then install a vinyl group at the meso‐position via a Wittig reaction. Upon UV irradiation the appended vinyl group cyclizes with the BODIPY core to form a strained cycloadduct that “stores” solar energy in its newly formed sigma bond; exposure to visible light (or gentle heating) reverses the cyclization, releasing the stored energy.

Synthesize a substituted Dewar benzene derivative by first converting benzene into 1,2,3,4‐tetra‑tert‑butylbenzene (via sequential Friedel–Crafts alkylations with tert‑butyl chloride) and then subjecting this aromatic compound to UV irradiation in a quartz reactor so that it undergoes a reversible valence isomerization into its strained Dewar benzene form—with the newly formed sigma bond “storing” solar energy that is released upon gentle thermal (or visible‐light) reversion to the aromatic state.

Synthesize a functionalized anthracene that forms its high‐energy 9,10‐endoperoxide under photooxidative conditions. For example, first prepare 9,10‐diphenylanthracene via a Friedel–Crafts acylation/reduction sequence, then irradiate its solution in the presence of a photosensitizer (such as rose bengal) and molecular oxygen to generate the corresponding 9,10‐endoperoxide. This endoperoxide stores solar energy in its strained O–O bond and reverts thermally to the parent anthracene, thereby releasing the stored energy.

Synthesize a 9,9‐dialkyl-2,7‐divinylfluorene by first oxidizing fluorene to its 2,7-diformyl derivative and then converting the aldehydes into vinyl groups via Wittig reactions; upon UV irradiation, the pendant vinyl groups undergo an intramolecular [2+2] cycloaddition to form a strained cyclobutane bridge that stores solar energy, which can be cleaved (by visible light or mild heating) to release the energy.

Synthesize a ruthenium nitrosyl complex that exploits photoinduced linkage isomerization—for example, prepare [RuCl(NO)(py)₄]PF₆ by first forming a Ru–pyridine precursor from RuCl₃ and pyridine, then introduce the NO unit via treatment with a nitrite source under acidic conditions. Upon UV irradiation, the NO ligand switches from its stable N‐bound configuration to a strained, higher‐energy O‐bound isomer that stores solar energy in the altered Ru–NO bond; exposure to visible light or gentle heating reverses the process, releasing the stored energy.

Synthesize a fulvalene diruthenium complex by first performing a McMurry coupling between two appropriately substituted aromatic aldehydes to form a conjugated fulvalene ligand, then reacting this ligand with Ru3(CO)12 under controlled thermal conditions to assemble the dinuclear complex; upon visible‐light irradiation, the complex undergoes a reversible isomerization that alters the Ru–Ru bonding (introducing a strained, high‐energy configuration), which thermally reverts to the original state to release the stored solar energy.

Synthesize a bis(cinnamate) derivative by esterifying trans‑cinnamic acid with a suitable diol to form a tethered molecule whose pendant olefins undergo a reversible intramolecular [2+2] photocycloaddition under UV light—creating a strained cyclobutane bond that stores solar energy and later reverts (upon visible‐light irradiation or mild heating) to release the energy.

Propose a metal surface, and a nanoparti.answer: A Pt(111) surface decorated with a truncated‐octahedral Pd nanoparticle—whose high‐index facets and edge sites create synergistic electronic and strain effects at the interface—is expected to show enhanced catalytic activity for reactions like oxygen reduction and hydrogen oxidation.

A Au(111) surface decorated with an icosahedral Cu nanoparticle—whose unique interfacial strain and electronic modulation enhance adsorption and activation of reactants—is proposed to boost catalytic performance in CO₂ reduction reactions.

A Ru(0001) surface decorated with a hemispherical Ir nanoparticle—whose strong interfacial strain and electronic coupling promote efficient water molecule activation—is proposed to enhance catalytic performance in water oxidation reactions.

A Ni(111) surface decorated with cuboctahedral Ru nanoparticles is proposed for enhanced methane steam reforming. In this design, the Ni substrate assists in methane adsorption and activation, while the well‐defined high‐index facets on the Ru particles induce interfacial strain and electronic modifications that promote water dissociation and reduce carbon buildup, together leading to improved catalytic performance.

An Ag(111) surface decorated with an octahedral Rh nanoparticle is proposed. In this design, the Ag support weakly binds reactants while modulating the electronic structure of the Rh nanoparticle’s facets, thereby generating interfacial sites that enhance oxygen activation and substrate adsorption—features that can boost performance in selective oxidation reactions.

A Co(0001) surface decorated with a decahedral Pt nanoparticle is proposed for enhanced oxygen reduction reaction catalysis. The Co support induces favorable strain and electronic modulation at the Pt interface, optimizing oxygen adsorption and activation for improved fuel cell performance.

A Cu(100) surface decorated with a truncated tetrahedral Au nanoparticle is proposed. In this design, the Cu(100) support promotes strong adsorption and activation of CO molecules, while the under-coordinated edges of the truncated tetrahedral Au nanoparticle facilitate oxygen activation. The interfacial electronic coupling and strain effects are expected to boost catalytic efficiency in CO oxidation reactions.

A Pd(111) surface decorated with a tetrahedral Au nanoparticle is proposed. The strong interfacial interaction between the Pd support and the uniquely structured Au particle is expected to induce electronic modifications at the low‐coordinated vertex and edge sites of the nanoparticle, thereby enhancing O₂ activation and tuning the adsorption energies of co-reactants. This synergy could lead to improved catalytic performance in oxidation reactions such as CO oxidation or selective alcohol oxidations.

A tungsten (W)(110) surface decorated with a concave nanocube‐shaped bimetallic Pt–Ni nanoparticle is proposed for enhanced selective hydrogenation catalysis. The strong metal–support interaction at the W interface induces interfacial strain and charge transfer that modify the nanoparticle’s d‐band structure, while the concave nanocube morphology provides abundant low‐coordination sites for efficient activation of hydrogen and unsaturated bonds—thereby promoting reactions such as the selective hydrogenation of α,β‐unsaturated aldehydes.

An Ir(111) surface decorated with an icosahedral Ni nanoparticle is proposed. The strong metal–metal interaction at the interface is expected to induce strain and electronic modifications on the high‐index facets of the Ni particle, thereby optimizing hydrogen activation and substrate binding. This synergy could enhance catalytic performance in selective alkyne hydrogenation reactions.

A Rh(111) surface decorated with a decahedral Co nanoparticle is proposed. The strong metal–metal interaction at the interface is expected to induce interfacial strain and electronic charge redistribution, with the under‐coordinated sites on the Co nanoparticle facilitating the dissociative activation of N₂ and H₂. This synergy could enhance catalytic performance in reactions such as ammonia synthesis.

A Fe(110) surface decorated with a star‐shaped Au nanoparticle is proposed. The Fe substrate induces charge redistribution and strain at the Au interface, which stabilizes high‐index, under‐coordinated sites along the nanoparticle’s arms and promotes the adsorption and activation of O₂ and CO—leading to enhanced catalytic activity for low‐temperature CO oxidation.

A rhenium (Re)(0001) surface decorated with a rhombic‑dodecahedral platinum (Pt) nanoparticle is proposed. The strong metal–metal interaction at the Pt/Re interface is expected to generate local strain and electronic modulation, exposing abundant high‐index sites that facilitate C–H bond activation. This synergy is particularly promising for enhancing dehydrogenation reactions, such as in light alkane reforming processes.

A molybdenum (Mo)(100) surface supporting nanocube‐shaped ruthenium (Ru) nanoparticles is proposed. In this design, the Mo(100) substrate can induce interfacial strain and subtle charge transfer to the Ru nanoparticle, thereby tuning its d‐band structure. Meanwhile, the well‐defined (100) facets of the Ru nanocubes present abundant low‐coordination edge and corner sites that are highly active for N₂ adsorption and H₂ dissociation. This synergistic metal–metal interface is expected to enhance catalytic performance in reactions such as ammonia synthesis.

A Pd(100) surface decorated with an icosahedral osmium (Os) nanoparticle is proposed for enhanced selective hydrogenation. Here, the Pd(100) support modulates the electronic structure and induces interfacial strain in the Os nanoparticle—especially at its high-index facets—thereby promoting efficient H₂ dissociation and optimized adsorbate binding for hydrogenation of unsaturated hydrocarbons.

A Au(110) surface decorated with a nanorod‐shaped Pd nanoparticle is proposed. The open, anisotropic structure of the Au(110) facet promotes directional strain and charge transfer to the Pd nanorod, effectively tuning its d‐band center. This synergistic interaction enhances the adsorption and activation of reactants, making the system particularly promising for oxidation processes such as formic acid oxidation.

A V(100) surface decorated with a nanoprism‐shaped platinum (Pt) nanoparticle is proposed. The vanadium substrate, through interfacial strain and charge redistribution, is expected to modulate the Pt nanoparticle’s electronic structure—particularly on its high‐energy facets—thereby enhancing the adsorption and activation of oxygen and organic intermediates for selective oxidation reactions.

A Au(100) surface decorated with a pentagonal bipyramid‐shaped Pt nanoparticle is proposed. In this design, the open Au(100) facet, which weakly binds reactants, promotes subtle charge redistribution at the nanoparticle–support interface; meanwhile, the unique pentagonal bipyramid shape of the Pt particle exposes a high density of under‐coordinated edge and vertex sites. This synergistic metal–metal interaction is expected to lower activation barriers and enhance catalytic efficiency in low‐temperature methanol oxidation, making it promising for fuel cell applications.

A Au(111) surface decorated with a nanocube‐shaped bimetallic Pt–Sn nanoparticle is proposed for enhanced electrocatalytic oxidation of alcohols. The Au(111) support induces subtle electron transfer and strain effects at the interface, while the Pt–Sn nanocube—with its well‐defined facets, edges, and corners—provides synergistic active sites that lower activation barriers for dehydrogenation and subsequent oxidation steps in alcohol fuel cells.

A Ti(0001) surface decorated with a nanoflower‐shaped bimetallic Pt–Co nanoparticle is proposed. In this design, the reactive Ti substrate interacts strongly with the nanoflower morphology—whose petal‐like protrusions expose a high density of low‐coordinated sites—and the Pt–Co alloying synergistically tunes the local electronic structure and strain at the interface. This combination is expected to lower activation barriers for reactions such as the oxygen reduction reaction in fuel cell applications.

A Cr(110) surface decorated with a branched, nanodendritic‐shaped bimetallic Ni–Cu nanoparticle is proposed. The Cr substrate is expected to induce interfacial strain and subtle charge transfer that modulates the Ni–Cu alloy’s electronic structure, while the nanodendritic morphology supplies a high density of under‐coordinated active sites. Together, these features could synergistically enhance the adsorption and activation of CO₂ and H₂, improving the performance of CO₂ hydrogenation to methanol.

A Nb(110) surface decorated with a Wulff‐shaped Ir nanoparticle is proposed for enhanced electrocatalytic activity in the oxygen evolution reaction. The Nb substrate induces tensile strain and subtle charge redistribution at the metal–nanoparticle interface, which shifts the Ir d‑band center and amplifies the exposure of high‐index, under‐coordinated facets. This synergy facilitates the adsorption and activation of oxygenated intermediates, thereby lowering reaction barriers and boosting overall catalytic efficiency.

A Cu(111) surface decorated with a tetrapod‐shaped Ag nanoparticle is proposed for enhanced electrocatalytic CO₂ reduction; the Cu substrate promotes effective CO₂ adsorption and activation, while the tetrapod’s high‐index and under‐coordinated facets synergistically lower reaction barriers by modulating the interfacial electronic structure.

A tantalum (Ta)(110) surface decorated with a cone‐shaped bimetallic Ni–Fe nanoparticle is proposed. The Ta support induces interfacial strain and charge transfer to the Ni–Fe particle, tuning its electronic structure, while the cone‐shaped morphology exposes a wealth of under‐coordinated sites that facilitate hydrogen adsorption and activation. This synergy is expected to lower energy barriers for key steps in the hydrogen evolution reaction.

A Pd(110) surface decorated with a nanostar‐shaped bimetallic Au–Cu nanoparticle is proposed. The open, anisotropic Pd(110) facet is expected to induce directional strain and electronic modulation at the interface, while the star‐like morphology of the Au–Cu particle exposes a high density of under‐coordinated, high‐index sites. This synergy can lower activation barriers and enhance the selective oxidation of alcohols.

A Rh(110) surface decorated with a spindle‐shaped bimetallic Pd–Sn nanoparticle is proposed. The anisotropic Rh(110) facet induces directional strain and electronic perturbation at the Pd–Sn interface, selectively exposing under‐coordinated sites that facilitate oxygen activation and dehydrogenation of ethanol. This synergy is expected to enhance electrocatalytic performance in direct ethanol fuel cell applications.

A Pt(110) surface decorated with a platelet‐shaped bimetallic Ni–Mo nanoparticle is proposed. The anisotropic Pt(110) facet induces interfacial strain and charge redistribution that finely tunes the Ni–Mo d‐band, while the flat, extended platelet morphology maximizes exposure of under‐coordinated edge sites. This synergy is expected to promote efficient water dissociation and optimize hydrogen adsorption/desorption kinetics, thereby enhancing the catalyst’s performance for the hydrogen evolution reaction in alkaline media.

A Cu(110) surface decorated with a hollow, nanocage‐shaped bimetallic Pd–Zn nanoparticle is proposed. The open Cu(110) facet promotes strong CO₂ adsorption, while the unique nanocage architecture of the Pd–Zn particle exposes a high density of under‐coordinated sites and induces intermetallic electronic effects that facilitate H₂ dissociation and CO₂ insertion—synergistically enhancing catalytic efficiency in CO₂ hydrogenation to methanol.

A Cu(311) surface decorated with a nanorice‐shaped bismuth (Bi) nanoparticle is proposed for enhanced electrochemical CO₂ reduction to formate. In this design, the stepped Cu(311) facet aids in strong CO₂ adsorption and activation, while the elongated nanorice morphology of the Bi particle exposes a high density of under‐coordinated sites that stabilize key reaction intermediates, thereby lowering activation barriers and improving selectivity toward formate formation.

A Zr(0001) surface decorated with a truncated‐octahedral bimetallic Ni–Ga nanoparticle. In this design, the electron‐rich Zr substrate promotes interfacial strain and charge transfer that modulates the d‑band structure of the Ni–Ga alloy, particularly at its under‐coordinated edge and corner sites. This synergy is expected to lower activation barriers for C–H bond cleavage, making the system especially promising for selective alkane dehydrogenation reactions.

A stepped Cu(211) surface decorated with a nanoplate‐shaped Sn nanoparticle is proposed. In this configuration, the under‐coordinated atoms on the Cu(211) surface enhance CO₂ adsorption and activation, while the ultrathin Sn nanoplate—with its abundant edge sites and modulated electronic structure—synergistically tunes reaction intermediates, potentially lowering barriers for the selective reduction of CO₂ to formate.

A Ag(110) surface decorated with a nanorod‐shaped bimetallic Pd–In nanoparticle is proposed. The anisotropic Ag(110) facet induces directional strain and subtle charge redistribution at the metal–nanoparticle interface, while the elongated Pd–In nanorod exposes abundant under‐coordinated edge sites that enhance hydrogen and unsaturated bond activation. This synergistic interplay is expected to lower reaction barriers and improve selectivity in alkyne hydrogenation processes.

A Pt(100) surface decorated with a concave, bipyramidal Ru nanoparticle is proposed for enhanced catalytic performance in the water–gas shift reaction. In this configuration, the open square symmetry of the Pt(100) support induces interfacial electronic modulation and strain on the Ru nanoparticle, while the nanoparticle’s concave bipyramidal shape exposes abundant high‐index edges and step sites that are particularly active in water dissociation and CO activation. This metal–metal synergy is expected to lower reaction barriers and improve turnover frequencies under mild operating conditions.

A Co(0001) surface decorated with a cuboctahedral Au nanoparticle is proposed. In this design, the hcp cobalt substrate induces interfacial strain and charge redistribution that modulates the d‐band structure of the Au nanoparticle. Meanwhile, the cuboctahedral geometry exposes a wealth of under‐coordinated edge and facet sites that enhance O₂ activation and CO adsorption, suggesting that this catalyst could achieve improved turnover in low‐temperature CO oxidation reactions.

Suggest a new class of metal-organic fra.answer: Photoresponsive Azobenzene-Integrated MOFs that use reversible cis–trans isomerization of azobenzene linkers to dynamically adjust pore sizes upon light stimulation.
Electro-switchable MOFs featuring redox-active ferrocene linkers that undergo reversible oxidation and reduction, thereby triggering conformational changes to dynamically modulate pore dimensions.
Thermoresponsive MOFs utilizing polymer-conjugated linkers—such as poly(N-isopropylacrylamide)—that undergo reversible coil–globule transitions around their LCST to dynamically modulate pore sizes.

Mechanically interlocked MOFs that integrate rotaxane-based linkers to enable controlled shuttling motions, thereby reversibly adjusting pore sizes in response to specific mechanical or chemical stimuli.
pH‐Responsive Imine‐Linked MOFs that reversibly modulate their pore sizes through hydrolysis and reformation of imine bonds under different pH conditions.

Magneto-switchable spin‐crossover MOFs that utilize Fe(II) or Co(II) centers capable of reversible high‐spin/low‐spin transitions under an applied magnetic field, thereby inducing subtle changes in the coordination geometry to dynamically regulate pore dimensions.
Dielectric Field‐Responsive MOFs featuring high‐dipole organic linkers that reorient under an applied electric field to reversibly modulate the pore structure.

Cation‐responsive Crown Ether‐Integrated MOFs that embed crown ether units within the linkers, enabling reversible alkali metal ion binding (e.g., Na⁺ or K⁺) to trigger conformational changes and dynamically switch the pore sizes.

Anion-responsive MOFs featuring urea-functionalized linkers that reversibly bind target anions (such as phosphate) to induce cooperative hydrogen-bond rearrangements, thereby dynamically modulating the framework’s pore dimensions.

Dissipative Fuel‐Driven MOFs that incorporate dynamically reversible covalent bonds which, upon activation by a transient chemical fuel, reconfigure their framework to reversibly switch between contracted and expanded pore states.

CO2‐responsive amine‐functionalized MOFs that reversibly form carbamate linkages upon CO2 uptake to contract their pores and release CO2 to expand, thereby dynamically tuning pore sizes in response to gas concentration.

Tautomerism‐Switchable MOFs featuring organic linkers that undergo reversible keto–enol tautomerization to adjust their geometry and pore dimensions in response to subtle environmental changes such as temperature or solvent polarity.

Ultrasound‐responsive MOFs that incorporate piezoelectric metal clusters to transduce acoustic energy into local mechanical strain, thereby triggering reversible conformational changes and dynamic modulation of pore sizes.
Chiroptical MOFs based on helicene-derived linkers that reversibly invert their helical configuration under circularly polarized light to dynamically modulate pore sizes.

Biohybrid DNA‐Integrated MOFs that employ aptamer linkers capable of undergoing reversible, sequence‐specific conformational changes, thereby dynamically switching pore sizes in response to complementary DNA strand binding.

Hydrostatic Pressure‐Responsive MOFs that incorporate flexible, hinge‐like linkers which reversibly contract under elevated pressure and expand when the pressure is released, dynamically tuning the pore dimensions.

Diels–Alder switchable MOFs that integrate organic linkers bearing latent diene and dienophile groups, enabling reversible cycloaddition/retro-cycloaddition reactions upon thermal activation to dynamically modulate pore dimensions.

Ionic strength‐responsive MOFs featuring zwitterionic linkers that reversibly reorganize via controlled ion‐pairing interactions—switching between contracted and expanded pore states in response to changes in surrounding electrolyte concentration.

Coordination‐mode‐switchable MOFs that incorporate ambidentate, flexible linkers—such as pyridine‐based derivatives—that reversibly toggle between chelating and bridging coordination geometries in response to environmental changes (e.g., solvent polarity or competitive guest binding), thereby dynamically modulating pore sizes.

Dual Interpenetration-Switchable MOFs that undergo reversible transitions between interpenetrated and open-framework states upon changes in solvent composition, thereby dynamically modulating their pore sizes.

Allosteric Effector-Responsive MOFs that embed secondary, flexible binding sites within their organic linkers—binding of a specific small-molecule effector triggers a cooperative conformational change that reversibly opens or contracts the pore channels.

Diarylethene‐Based Photoresponsive MOFs that incorporate diarylethene linkers undergoing reversible cyclization under alternating UV and visible light irradiation, thereby dynamically modulating the framework’s pore size.

Boronate Ester‐Exchange MOFs that incorporate linkers bearing boronic acid groups which reversibly form and break boronate ester bonds with diol-functionalized co-linkers—allowing the framework to switch between contracted and expanded pore sizes in response to humidity or diol stimuli.

Liquid crystal-integrated MOFs that incorporate mesogenic linkers capable of reversibly switching between ordered and disordered phases under external stimuli (such as electric fields or temperature changes), thereby dynamically tuning the pore sizes.

Metal Cluster Reconfigurable MOFs (MCR‐MOFs) that exploit the reversible isomerization of polymetallic cluster nodes—switching between distinct geometries (e.g., octahedral versus trigonal prismatic)—to dynamically alter linker orientations and pore dimensions in response to stimuli such as temperature or solvent polarity.

Halogen-bond-responsive MOFs that feature flexible linkers decorated with halogen bond donor and acceptor groups, enabling reversible pore size switching upon interaction with specific halogen bonding partners.

Redox‐Responsive Disulfide‐Exchange MOFs that incorporate flexible linkers bearing disulfide bonds, which reversibly break and reform under mild redox conditions to dynamically tune the pore dimensions.

Plasmonic Nanoparticle‐Enhanced MOFs that embed gold nanorods to locally convert visible light into heat, triggering reversible thermal transitions in nearby responsive linkers to dynamically modulate pore dimensions.
Cyclodextrin-functionalized MOFs that harness reversible host–guest interactions of pendant cyclodextrin units to dynamically modulate pore sizes upon binding or releasing small organic guests.

Enzyme‐mediated Phosphorylation‐Switchable MOFs: frameworks featuring peptide linkers with phosphorylatable residues that reversibly expand or contract pore sizes upon selective kinase‐driven phosphorylation and phosphatase‐catalyzed dephosphorylation.

Reversible Olefin Metathesis‐Switchable MOFs: frameworks built with terminal olefin–functionalized linkers that can undergo catalyst‐mediated ring‐closing olefin metathesis to reversibly shorten the linker length—thereby contracting the pores—and then reform to the extended state to expand the pore sizes.

Metal Ion‐Exchange Switchable MOFs that feature labile metal nodes capable of reversible ion substitution—altering the nodes’ coordination geometry and thereby dynamically modulating the pore sizes in response to specific metal ion stimuli.

Spiropyran-Integrated MOFs: frameworks incorporating spiropyran moieties into the organic linkers that reversibly switch between a closed, nonpolar state and an open, polar merocyanine form under UV/visible light, thereby dynamically altering pore sizes through changes in steric hindrance and intermolecular interactions.

Light‐activated proton‐relay MOFs featuring photoacid‐functionalized linkers that, upon irradiation, undergo reversible proton release to trigger cooperative hydrogen‐bond network reorganization and dynamically adjust the pore dimensions.

Twistronic Layered MOFs: frameworks built from reconfigurable 2D layers whose relative twist angles can be reversibly adjusted (e.g., via mechanical shear or solvent switching) to dynamically modulate the size of the pore channels.

DASAs‐Based MOFs: frameworks that incorporate donor–acceptor Stenhouse adduct moieties into their organic linkers, enabling a reversible, visible light‐triggered switch between an open, linear configuration and a closed, cyclic form that dynamically alters the pore sizes.
Molecular Motor‐Driven MOFs that incorporate overcrowded alkene‐based linkers, which undergo unidirectional, light‐induced rotary motion to reversibly switch between open and contracted pore states.

Foldamer‐Integrated MOFs: frameworks that incorporate synthetic peptide‐foldamer linkers capable of reversibly switching between compact (folded) and extended conformations in response to external triggers (such as light, temperature, or solvent polarity), thereby dynamically modulating the effective pore dimensions.

A self‐oscillatory Belousov–Zhabotinsky (BZ) MOF design that embeds redox‐active catalysts into its metal nodes and linkers, enabling autonomous, periodic oxidation‐state oscillations which reversibly switch the framework’s pore dimensions without constant external triggers.

Photoinduced [2+2] Cycloaddition MOFs that employ alkene‐terminated organic linkers which reversibly dimerize under UV light to contract the pore network and dissociate under visible light to reopen it.

Viologen‐Integrated Electrochromic MOFs that employ redox‐active bipyridinium linkers which, upon electrochemical reduction, form reversible π–stacked radical dimers to trigger conformational rearrangements, dynamically switching the framework between expanded and contracted pore states.

Axial‐Ligand Coordinated MOFs: frameworks engineered with labile metal nodes possessing open axial sites that reversibly bind small external ligands—triggering a switch between distinct coordination geometries and, in turn, dynamically modulating the pore dimensions in response to specific chemical stimuli.

Frustrated Lewis Pair‐Integrated MOFs—frameworks whose organic linkers incorporate both Lewis acidic and basic sites that reversibly form intramolecular adducts (triggered by specific small molecules or subtle thermal changes) to switch the pore dimensions between open and contracted states.

Haptotropic Rearrangement‐Induced MOFs: frameworks integrating indenyl‐based organometallic linkers that, upon a mild redox trigger, reversibly switch their binding mode (from η⁵ to η³), thereby reorganizing the metal node geometry and dynamically modulating the pore sizes.

Jahn–Teller Distortion-Activated MOFs: frameworks that incorporate Jah–Teller active metal centers (such as Cu²⁺) whose reversible distortion between elongated and compressed coordination geometries—triggered by mild thermal or electric field stimuli—dynamically modulates the pore sizes.

Quinone–Hydroquinone Redox–Switchable MOFs: frameworks incorporating redox–active quinone linkers that reversibly convert to hydroquinone under controlled electrochemical conditions, altering hydrogen bonding and steric interactions to dynamically modulate pore sizes.

N‐heterocyclic Carbene (NHC)–Integrated MOFs that employ flexible, redox‐responsive NHC linkers capable of reversible coordination mode reorientation under electrochemical stimulation to dynamically tune the pore dimensions.

Catalyst‐Activated Hydrogenation‐Switchable MOFs: frameworks built with unsaturated organic linkers that, under controlled H₂ exposure in the presence of a suitable catalyst, reversibly hydrogenate (and dehydrogenate) to switch between a rigid, contracted state and a flexible, expanded pore conformation.

Superlattice-Assembled MOF Supercrystals, where nanoscale MOF particles self-organize into reversible superlattices that adjust interparticle spacing—and thus overall pore sizes—in response to external stimuli such as solvent composition or magnetic fields.

Terahertz‐Activated Vibroflex MOFs that utilize low‐frequency linker vibrational modes—selectively excited by terahertz radiation—to reversibly induce conformational distortions and dynamically switch pore sizes.

Dendritic Linker MOFs—frameworks that incorporate hyperbranched, dendrimer-inspired organic linkers which reversibly fold or extend in response to environmental changes (e.g., variations in solvent polarity or pH), thereby dynamically “zipping” and “unzipping” the pore channels to tune their sizes.

Luminescent AIE‐Active MOFs that integrate tetraphenylethylene (TPE)–based linkers; upon exposure to specific wavelengths, the TPE units switch reversibly between twisted and planar conformations, dynamically modulating pore sizes while concurrently providing an optical readout of the transition.

Design MOFs incorporating nitroxide-functionalized linkers that reversibly form radical dimers under controlled redox or thermal conditions, thereby reconfiguring the framework’s geometry and dynamically switching pore sizes.

NO‐Responsive Heme‐Mimetic MOFs that incorporate iron–porphyrin nodes, which reversibly bind nitric oxide to switch the metal’s coordination geometry and spin state, thereby dynamically modulating the pore sizes.

Ionic Liquid–Tethered MOFs that incorporate flexible, tethered ionic liquid side‐chains into the linkers, which can reversibly rearrange under thermal or electric field stimuli to modulate the framework’s pore dimensions.

Chalcogen-Bond Switchable MOFs: frameworks incorporating organic linkers with selenoether or telluroether groups that reversibly form chalcogen bonds with guest or intraframework acceptors, thereby triggering conformational changes to dynamically modulate pore sizes.

Calixarene‐Integrated MOFs that employ flexible calixarene-based linkers, which reversibly switch between distinct conformations (e.g., cone versus partial‐cone) in response to guest binding or solvent changes to dynamically tune the pore sizes.

Photoactivated Metal‐to‐Ligand Charge Transfer (MLCT) Switchable MOFs that integrate Ru(II) or Ir(III) centers with flexible, photoactive linkers which, upon light irradiation, undergo MLCT‐induced changes in coordination geometry and bond lengths to reversibly modulate pore sizes.

Cucurbituril‐Gated MOFs: frameworks that integrate cucurbit[7]uril‐functionalized linkers, enabling reversible host–guest inclusion of ammonium guests to dynamically modulate pore apertures in response to chemical stimuli.

Fluoride‐Responsive Silyl Ether MOFs: frameworks designed with organic linkers that incorporate silyl ether groups which reversibly cleave and reform upon exposure to fluoride ions, thereby dynamically modulating the pore dimensions.

Valence tautomerism‐induced MOFs featuring redox‐active catecholate linkers coordinated to cobalt centers that reversibly switch between distinct valence states—thereby altering the metal–ligand bond geometry and dynamically modulating pore sizes in response to light or thermal stimuli.

Aromaticity‐Switchable MOFs that incorporate conjugated linkers capable of undergoing reversible photoinduced transitions between aromatic and antiaromatic states, thereby altering linker rigidity and electronic structure to dynamically modulate pore sizes.

Bridged Node MOFs – frameworks designed with metal nodes bearing pendant coordination sites that reversibly bind an external bidentate bridging ligand to draw adjacent nodes together, contracting the pore channels; removal of the ligand restores the open framework and dynamically adjusts the pore size.

ESIPT‐Responsive MOFs – frameworks that incorporate linkers based on salicylidene‐malononitrile motifs which, upon UV excitation, undergo an excited‐state intramolecular proton transfer (switching rapidly between enol and keto forms) to reversibly modulate the framework’s pore dimensions.

Suggest a catalytic system for convertin.answer: A Zn–salen catalyst that promotes the cycloaddition of CO2 with epoxides to form cyclic carbonates under ambient conditions.
A cobalt phthalocyanine catalyst immobilized on carbon nanotubes for electrocatalytic CO2 reduction to CO at ambient temperature and pressure.
A bismuth nanocatalyst supported on carbon for electrocatalytically reducing CO₂ to formate under ambient conditions.

A dual photoredox/nickel catalytic system—employing an organic photosensitizer like 4CzIPN together with a Ni(bipyridine) complex to enable the carboxylation of aryl halides with CO₂ into aromatic carboxylic acids under ambient conditions.
A copper nanoparticle catalyst dispersed on nitrogen-doped graphene that electrocatalytically reduces CO₂ to ethanol under ambient conditions.

A frustrated Lewis pair system combining tris(pentafluorophenyl)borane with a bulky phosphine (e.g., P(tBu)₃) to catalyze the hydrosilylation of CO₂ into methoxysilanes—which can be hydrolyzed to methanol—operating under ambient temperature and pressure.

A nickel–NHC catalyst (e.g., a Ni(IPr) complex used with a mild organic base) that mediates the carboxylative cyclization of propargyl amines with CO₂ to yield 2‑oxazolidinones under ambient conditions.
A silver–N‐heterocyclic carbene (NHC) complex that catalyzes the direct carboxylation of terminal alkynes with CO₂ to produce propiolic acids under ambient temperature and pressure.
A homogeneous manganese(I) tricarbonyl bipyridine complex (e.g., Mn(bpy)(CO)₃Br) used in an electrocatalytic system to selectively reduce CO₂ to CO under ambient temperature and pressure.

A biohybrid photocatalytic system that couples carbon monoxide dehydrogenase (CODH) with a graphitic carbon nitride (g-C₃N₄) semiconductor to convert CO₂ into acetic acid under visible‐light irradiation at ambient temperature and pressure.

A dinuclear aluminum complex featuring a bulky bis(phenolate) ligand that catalyzes the alternating copolymerization of CO₂ with cyclohexene oxide to yield poly(cyclohexene carbonate) under ambient conditions.
A ruthenium PNP pincer complex (e.g., RuH(CO)(PNP)) that catalytically hydrogenates CO₂ to methanol in the presence of H₂ and a mild base under ambient temperature and pressure.

A palladium catalyst paired with a bidentate phosphine ligand (e.g., Pd(OAc)₂/dppf) for the direct carboxylation of arylboronic acids with CO₂ to form aromatic carboxylic acids at ambient temperature and pressure.
A visible‐light photoelectrochemical system featuring single‐atom iron centers anchored on nitrogen‐doped graphene that selectively reduces CO₂ to methane in aqueous media under ambient conditions.
A homogeneous Ir(POCOP) hydride catalyst system that uses H₂ and a mild base to hydrogenate CO₂ to formic acid under ambient temperature and pressure.

A Cp*Rh(III) catalyst—such as [Cp*RhCl₂]₂ activated with a mild base (e.g., CsF)—that promotes the direct C–H carboxylation of arenes with CO₂ to form aromatic carboxylic acids under ambient temperature and pressure.

A metal‐free covalent organic framework (COF) incorporating electron‐rich triarylamine units that, under visible‐light irradiation with a suitable sacrificial electron donor, selectively reduces CO₂ to carbon monoxide at ambient temperature and pressure.
A lanthanum‐based metal–organic framework integrated with a visible‐light photosensitizer and sacrificial electron donor to capture CO₂ and photoreduce it to formate under ambient conditions.
A photocatalytic system based on a vanadium‐substituted silicotungstate polyoxometalate paired with a visible‐light sensitizer to convert CO₂ into oxalate under ambient conditions.
A bimetallic nickel–iron layered double hydroxide electrocatalyst that converts CO₂ into syngas (a controlled CO/H₂ mixture) under ambient temperature and pressure.

A homogeneous rhenium(I) tricarbonyl bipyridine complex (e.g., fac-Re(bpy)(CO)₃Cl) combined with a sacrificial electron donor under visible‐light irradiation to photocatalytically reduce CO₂ to CO at ambient temperature and pressure.
A heterogeneous tin(IV) catalyst supported on mesoporous silica that enables the direct synthesis of dimethyl carbonate from CO₂ and methanol at ambient temperature and pressure.

A photoelectrochemical system featuring mesoporous TiO₂ sensitized with a perylene diimide dye and decorated with nickel nanoparticles that selectively reduces CO₂ to methanol under visible‐light irradiation at ambient temperature and pressure.

A homogeneous N‐heterocyclic carbene‐stabilized zinc hydride complex that catalyzes the hydroboration of CO₂ with pinacolborane to form a boryl formate intermediate, which can be hydrolyzed to methanol under ambient conditions.
A bimetallic indium–copper electrocatalyst that co‐activates CO₂ and ammonia to directly produce urea under ambient temperature and pressure.

A copper‐hydride catalyst system—in which a Cu(I) salt is activated by an N‐heterocyclic carbene and a hydrosilane—to promote the reductive carboxylation of imines with CO₂, yielding α‐amino acid derivatives under ambient conditions.
A tin(IV) porphyrin complex that mediates the [3+2] cycloaddition of CO₂ with aziridines to form oxazolidinones under ambient conditions.

A dual catalytic system that employs 9‐mesityl‐10‐methylacridinium tetrafluoroborate as a visible‐light photoredox catalyst in combination with a copper(I) complex (e.g., CuCl coordinated with a bidentate nitrogen ligand) to promote the hydrocarboxylation of alkenes with CO₂ into carboxylic acids under ambient temperature and pressure.

A single‐atom palladium catalyst supported on graphitic carbon nitride that, under visible‐light irradiation with an appropriate sacrificial electron donor, mediates the photoreduction of CO₂ to methanol at ambient temperature and pressure.

A homogeneous molybdenum PNP pincer complex (e.g., Mo(PNP)(CO)₂(H)₂) that catalyzes the hydrogenative amidation of CO₂ with amines to form N‐formyl amides under ambient temperature and pressure using H₂ as the reductant.
A nanostructured oxide‐derived copper electrocatalyst engineered with abundant grain boundaries to selectively reduce CO₂ to ethylene in aqueous media under ambient conditions.
A homogeneous nickel(II) bipyridine complex that electrocatalytically carboxylates benzyl halides with CO₂ to form phenylacetic acids under ambient temperature and pressure.

A homogeneous copper(I)–NHC catalyst system—for example, a Cu(I) complex bearing an imidazol-2-ylidene ligand—that promotes the carboxylative coupling of allylic halides with CO₂ to form allyl carboxylic acids at ambient temperature and pressure.

A heterogeneous electrocatalytic system featuring an iron porphyrin complex immobilized on a conductive polymer support—with pendent amine groups to assist proton transfer—that selectively reduces CO₂ to formate at ambient temperature and pressure.

A metal‐free bifunctional catalytic system combining 1,5,7‐triazabicyclo[4.4.0]dec‐5‐ene (TBD) with tetrabutylammonium bromide (TBAB) to promote the cycloaddition of CO₂ with epoxides into cyclic carbonates at ambient temperature and pressure.

A homogeneous cobalt(II) bis(imino)pyridine complex, used with a mild base such as KOtBu to promote the direct C–H carboxylation of imidazoles with CO₂ to furnish imidazole-2-carboxylic acids under ambient temperature and pressure.

A nanostructured bismuth‐doped tin oxide (Bi–SnO₂) electrocatalyst—deposited as a thin film on a conductive substrate—that selectively reduces CO₂ to formaldehyde in aqueous media under ambient temperature and pressure.
A bimetallic palladium–indium electrocatalyst immobilized on a carbon support that selectively reduces CO₂ to acetaldehyde in aqueous media at ambient temperature and pressure.
A single‐atom copper catalyst anchored on nitrogen‐doped carbon nanotubes that electrocatalytically reduces CO₂ to n‑propanol at ambient temperature and pressure.
A single‐atom cobalt catalyst supported on phosphorus‐doped graphene that electrocatalytically converts CO₂ to isopropanol under ambient temperature and pressure.

A single‐atom manganese catalyst anchored on graphitic carbon nitride (g‑C₃N₄) that, under visible‐light irradiation with a sacrificial electron donor, photocatalytically reduces CO₂ to formic acid at ambient temperature and pressure.

A homogeneous chromium(III) complex featuring a redox‐active terpyridine ligand paired with a visible‐light photosensitizer (e.g., eosin Y) and a sacrificial electron donor to photoreduce CO₂ into carbon monoxide at ambient temperature and pressure.

A heterogeneous electrocatalytic system featuring atomically dispersed tungsten centers on nitrogen‐doped carbon (W–N–C) that selectively converts CO₂ into n‑butanol at ambient temperature and pressure.

A dual photocatalytic system combining lead‐free Cs₂AgBiBr₆ perovskite quantum dots as visible‐light sensitizers with a cobalt–bipyridine complex to selectively reduce CO₂ to formate under ambient conditions.

A homogeneous copper(I)–N‑heterocyclic carbene catalyst (e.g., Cu–IMes) used with a mild base to promote the carboxylative coupling of arylboronic acids, CO₂, and amines for the selective synthesis of aryl carbamates at ambient temperature and pressure.

A heterogeneous plasmonic photocatalyst composed of gold nanoparticles supported on TiO₂, which under visible‐light irradiation selectively reduces CO₂ to methanol at ambient temperature and pressure.
A heterogeneous electrocatalytic system employing copper–zinc alloy nanoparticles supported on carbon nanotubes to selectively reduce CO₂ to acetone at ambient temperature and pressure.

A heterogeneous electrocatalytic system based on a porous polymer network embedded with single‐atom cobalt sites that selectively reduces CO₂ to propionic acid in aqueous media under ambient temperature and pressure.

A heterogeneous photocatalytic system based on CeO₂ nanoparticles decorated with single‐atom ruthenium sites, which under visible‐light irradiation in ethanol selectively converts CO₂ into ethyl formate at ambient temperature and pressure.

A homogeneous CAAC–iron(II) complex that catalyzes the hydrosilylation of CO₂ to generate silyl acetal intermediates—which can be readily hydrolyzed to ethylene glycol—at ambient temperature and pressure.

A heterogeneous electrocatalytic system comprising molybdenum carbide (Mo₂C) nanoparticles supported on reduced graphene oxide, which selectively converts CO₂ to methanol in aqueous media under ambient temperature and pressure.

A heterogeneous photocatalytic system comprising palladium nanoparticles supported on TiO₂ that, under visible‐light irradiation, converts CO₂ and methanol into methyl formate at ambient temperature and pressure.

A dual catalytic system that employs 4CzIPN as a visible‐light photoredox catalyst, quinuclidine as a hydrogen‐atom transfer mediator, and a NiCl₂/dtbbpy complex to enable the direct carboxylation of unactivated aliphatic C–H bonds with CO₂ into aliphatic carboxylic acids at ambient temperature and pressure.

Invent a way to combine electron-beams a.answer: I propose a hybrid Electron-Beam Assisted Scanning Probe Microscope (EB-SPM) that integrates a miniaturized electron-beam emitter directly into the apex of a scanning probe tip. In this system, the focused electron beam locally excites or modifies the sample—triggering secondary electron emission or inducing nanoscale chemical changes—while the same tip simultaneously maps topography, electrical, or magnetic properties with high spatial resolution. Coordinated beam modulation with probe feedback enables real‐time, multimodal analysis and manipulation at the nanoscale, advancing nanoscience through precise in situ imaging and fabrication.

I propose a dual-modality instrument where a precision external electron beam is synchronized with a scanning probe that features integrated electron detectors. In this system, the external beam irradiates the sample while the probe concurrently detects secondary electron emission and measures local electrical or mechanical signals. By correlating these signals through real-time feedback, the device enables simultaneous nanoscale imaging, controlled modification, and in situ analysis—advancing nanoscience with high spatial and functional resolution.

Propose a new material equivalent to car.answer: We propose a material called silicon phosphide nanotubes (SiPNTs), which are one‐dimensional tubular nanostructures built by rolling a single‐atom–thick, hexagonally coordinated SiP sheet (analogous to a silicon–phosphorene “graphene”) into a seamless tube. Their unique alternating Si–P bonding network is predicted to provide mechanical strength, tunable bandgaps, and high thermal stability similar to carbon nanotubes but without any carbon. To fabricate them, a chemical vapor deposition (CVD) technique can be employed: a nanoporous template (e.g., anodic aluminum oxide) is pre‐decorated with nanoscale metal catalyst particles, and a controlled gas mixture of silane (SiH4) and phosphine (PH3) is introduced at high temperatures (around 850–950 °C) in an inert atmosphere. Under these conditions, a two‐dimensional SiP layer forms on the catalyst and spontaneously curls into tubular structures; a subsequent annealing step promotes crystallinity and purity. This method yields SiP nanotubes with properties analogous to carbon nanotubes yet completely free of carbon.

We propose “germanium selenide nanotubes” (GeSeNTs): one‐dimensional, tubular nanostructures formed by rolling a single-atom–thick, hexagonally coordinated germanium selenide (GeSe) sheet. Their alternating Ge–Se bonds are expected to provide exceptional mechanical strength and tunable semiconducting behavior similar to carbon nanotubes. To synthesize them, a template-assisted chemical vapor deposition method can be used. In this process, a nanoporous substrate (such as anodic alumina) is first decorated with suitable metal catalyst nanoparticles. A controlled gas mixture of germanium tetrachloride (GeCl₄) and hydrogen selenide (H₂Se) is then introduced at temperatures around 700–800 °C under an inert atmosphere. Under these conditions, a two-dimensional GeSe layer forms on the catalyst sites and spontaneously curls into nanotubular structures; a subsequent annealing step improves crystallinity and stability. This method provides a carbon-free nanotube with properties analogous to those of carbon nanotubes.

We propose Boron Arsenide Nanotubes (BAsNTs)—one‐dimensional, tubular nanostructures formed by rolling a novel, single‐atom–thick BAs honeycomb sheet (with alternating boron and arsenic atoms) into a seamless tube. Their unique B–As bonding is expected to yield high mechanical strength, tunable electronic behavior, and excellent thermal conductivity analogous to carbon nanotubes but completely devoid of carbon. To synthesize BAsNTs, a template‐assisted chemical vapor deposition (CVD) method can be employed: a nanoporous anodic aluminum oxide template is first seeded with catalytic nanoparticles; then, under an inert atmosphere at 800–900 °C, a controlled mixture of boron trichloride (BCl₃) and arsine (AsH₃) gases reacts on the nanopore walls to form a continuous, monolayer BAs film that spontaneously curls into tubular structures. A subsequent annealing step improves crystallinity, and the sacrificial template is later etched away to release free‐standing BAs nanotubes.

We propose gallium phosphide nanotubes (GaPNTs), which are one‐atom–thick GaP sheets arranged in a honeycomb lattice and seamlessly rolled into tubes. Their alternating Ga–P bonds are expected to impart high mechanical strength, tunable semiconducting behavior, and thermal stability similar to carbon nanotubes without involving any carbon. To synthesize GaPNTs, a template-assisted metalorganic chemical vapor deposition (MOCVD) method can be employed: first, a nanoporous anodic aluminum oxide scaffold is pre-seeded with nanoscale catalyst particles; then, using trimethylgallium and phosphine as precursors in an inert atmosphere at 700–800 °C, a two-dimensional GaP monolayer is deposited on the template walls.
